syllabus_desc: Describe the basic characteristics of High Frequency (HF) and Very High Frequency (VHF) in terms of: Range
concept: VHF radio operates on frequencies between 118-136.975 MHz and provides line-of-sight communication. The range is limited by the radio horizon, which extends with altitude. An aircraft at 5,000 feet may communicate with ground stations over 40-50 nm away. HF radio operates on frequencies between 3-30 MHz and uses sky wave propagation, bouncing signals off the ionosphere, allowing long-range communication over hundreds of nautical miles. This makes HF essential for oceanic and remote area flights.
exam_tip: VHF = line-of-sight (altitude-dependent range). HF = sky wave via ionosphere (long-range). Know the frequency bands.
key_facts: VHF frequency range: 118-136.975 MHz
VHF range extends with altitude
HF frequency range: 3-30 MHz
HF uses ionospheric reflection for long-range comms
Radio horizon expands approximately 1.2 times geometric horizon

syllabus_desc: Describe the basic characteristics of High Frequency (HF) and Very High Frequency (VHF) in terms of: Surface interference
concept: Terrain obstacles create communication 'shadows' where radio signals cannot propagate effectively. In New Zealand, the Southern Alps and other mountainous regions can block VHF signals, requiring aircraft to climb to higher altitudes to establish communication. HF signals are affected by ionospheric conditions, solar activity (sunspot cycles), and atmospheric disturbances like thunderstorms. Line-of-sight blockage is the primary limitation for VHF; propagation delays and static are the main concerns for HF.
exam_tip: VHF blocked by terrain—climb higher. HF affected by ionosphere conditions and solar activity.
key_facts: Mountains and terrain create communication shadows
NZ Southern Alps require altitude for VHF coverage
HF propagation varies with sunspot activity
Thunderstorms degrade HF signal quality
Time of day affects HF propagation

syllabus_desc: Describe the basic characteristics of High Frequency (HF) and Very High Frequency (VHF) in terms of: Clarity of reception
concept: VHF provides exceptionally clear, static-free reception when operating within range, due to the high frequency and short wavelength. This clarity makes VHF the preferred medium for domestic NZ operations. HF reception is typically noisier, with more background static and interference, but the long-range capability is essential for oceanic flights. NZ uses 25 kHz channel spacing for aviation VHF, giving frequencies like 118.100, 118.125, etc. Some European airspace uses 8.33 kHz spacing for more channels, but this is not used in NZ.
exam_tip: VHF = clear, static-free. HF = noisier but long-range. NZ uses 25 kHz channel spacing.
key_facts: VHF provides high-quality, clear audio
HF reception includes more background noise
NZ uses 25 kHz channel spacing
Frequencies expressed to 3 decimal places (e.g., 118.100)
VHF preferred for domestic NZ operations

syllabus_desc: VHF radio controls: volume, squelch, frequency selection, PTT
concept: VHF radio transceivers have essential controls: the volume knob adjusts audio output level, the squelch control silences background noise when no signal is being received (prevents static from being heard), the frequency selector allows tuning to active and standby frequencies (dual VHF radios are standard in NZ aircraft), and the PTT (push-to-talk) button on the control yoke transmits when pressed. Understanding squelch is critical—set too high and weak signals are lost; set too low and background noise is annoying.
exam_tip: Squelch = silence when no signal. Active frequency for transmit/receive; standby for monitoring. PTT on yoke.
key_facts: Volume knob controls audio level
Squelch silences static between transmissions
Active frequency transmits and receives
Standby frequency allows quick selection
PTT (Press-to-Talk) button is on control yoke

syllabus_desc: Intercom systems and audio selector panels
concept: Aircraft intercom systems allow crew to communicate internally. Two main types: voice-activated (hot mic) where the microphone is always live, requiring discipline to avoid transmitting private conversations; and PTT intercom where crew press a button to talk on intercom. It is critical to distinguish between intercom PTT and radio PTT—transmitting on radio when intending to use intercom creates embarrassing (and hazardous) situations. Audio selector panels allow switching between COM (communication radio), NAV (navigation radio), and other sources.
exam_tip: Don't confuse intercom PTT with radio PTT. Hot mic requires discipline. Audio selector switches between COM and NAV.
key_facts: Voice-activated (hot mic) intercom always listens
PTT intercom requires button press
Intercom PTT and radio PTT are different switches
Audio selector panel routes audio to headsets
COM selector tunes radio; NAV selector tunes navigation

syllabus_desc: Headset and microphone use
concept: Aviation headsets consist of two earpieces and a boom microphone. The boom microphone should be positioned so it just touches the pilot's lips, allowing clear transmission without excess background noise. Sidetone is the feedback of your own voice to your ears, confirming that audio is being transmitted. A hand-held microphone serves as backup in case the boom mic fails. Proper headset use is essential for clear communications and fatigue reduction on long flights.
exam_tip: Boom mic positioned at lips for clarity. Sidetone = your voice in your ears. Hand-held mic is backup.
key_facts: Boom microphone positioned to touch lips
Sidetone provides feedback of own transmissions
Headsets reduce ambient noise and pilot fatigue
Hand-held microphone serves as backup
Microphone placement affects transmission clarity

syllabus_desc: Describe the correct operation of: The headset/boom microphone combination;
concept: The headset/boom microphone is the standard setup in most GA aircraft. The headset provides clear two-way communication while keeping hands free for flight controls. The boom microphone should be positioned 1–2 cm from the mouth corner (not directly in front) to minimize breath noise and maximise clarity. Proper positioning and secure fitting are critical for transmitting intelligible messages to ATC.
exam_tip: Remember: boom mic should be 1–2 cm from mouth corner, not directly in front. This reduces breathing noise and wind rumble.
key_facts: Headset provides simultaneous listening and transmitting capability
Boom microphone positioned 1–2 cm from mouth corner
Correct positioning reduces breath noise and wind rumble
Secure fitting prevents microphone movement during flight
Push-to-talk (PTT) button must be within easy reach

syllabus_desc: Describe the correct operation of: The handheld microphone
concept: The handheld microphone is an alternative to the headset/boom setup, though less common in GA. It requires one hand to hold and operate, making it impractical during critical flight phases. When used, the microphone should be held 5–10 cm from the mouth at a slight angle (45°) to minimise breath noise. Clear articulation is essential since handheld operation often results in poorer transmission quality than boom microphones.
exam_tip: Handheld mics are rare in GA but if used: hold 5–10 cm from mouth at 45° angle. Not practical during critical flight phases.
key_facts: Handheld microphone requires one hand to operate
Impractical for use during critical flight phases (takeoff, landing, emergency)
Correct distance: 5–10 cm from mouth
Hold at 45° angle to minimise breath noise
Clear articulation critical due to poorer signal quality

syllabus_desc: Transponder controls: function selector, code selector, IDENT button
concept: Aircraft transponders are radar beacons that enhance visibility on ATC radar. The function selector has four positions: OFF (no transmission), STBY (standby—receiver active, no transmission), ON (continuously transmits selected code), and ALT (altitude—transmits altitude encoding from aircraft altimeter). The four-digit code selector (0-7 range per digit) allows pilots to set their unique transponder code. The IDENT button causes a distinctive blip on radar for 15 seconds, helping ATC positively identify the aircraft.
exam_tip: STBY = ready. ON = transmit code. ALT = transmit code + altitude. IDENT = radar blip for 15 sec.
key_facts: Function selector: OFF, STBY, ON, ALT
Code is 4 digits (0-7 range each digit)
ALT mode transmits altitude from encoding altimeter
IDENT button creates 15-second radar blip
Mode A transmits code only

syllabus_desc: Emergency transponder code 7700
concept: Transponder code 7700 indicates a general emergency—grave and imminent danger where immediate assistance is required. This code alerts ATC immediately and triggers audible warnings in ATC control rooms. Setting code 7700 should be accompanied by radio distress calls (MAYDAY) on the current frequency or 121.5 MHz. ATC will immediately prioritize radar separation and vectors to suitable landing facilities.
exam_tip: 7700 = General emergency. Always transmit MAYDAY on radio when setting 7700.
key_facts: Code 7700 indicates general emergency
Alerts ATC immediately with audible warning
Set 7700 and transmit MAYDAY on 121.5 MHz
ATC provides immediate priority handling
Indicates aircraft in grave and imminent danger

syllabus_desc: Radio failure code 7600
concept: Transponder code 7600 indicates radio failure or malfunction. When VHF radio fails, the pilot is unable to communicate with ATC via radio. Setting code 7600 alerts ATC to the radio failure, and ATC will then provide vectors using visual signals or radar. In NZ, if radio fails, maintain conspicuous flight path, stay outside controlled airspace if possible, and set transponder 7600 immediately. Some aircraft have alternative communication methods (mobile phone, secondary HF radio) to contact ATC.
exam_tip: 7600 = Radio failure. Set immediately, comply with ATC radar vectors, try alternate comms.
key_facts: Code 7600 indicates radio failure
ATC provides vectors without voice communication
Maintain conspicuous flight path
Avoid entering controlled airspace if possible
Try alternate comms (phone, HF, secondary radio)

syllabus_desc: Unlawful interference code 7500
concept: Transponder code 7500 indicates unlawful interference (hijacking, illegal interference, security threat). This code alerts ATC and military authorities. The pilot should set 7500 and continue flight to nearest suitable aerodrome. Military assistance may be provided. This is a serious emergency code and should only be set if there is genuine unlawful interference aboard the aircraft.
exam_tip: 7500 = Unlawful interference (hijacking). Set 7500 and land at nearest suitable aerodrome.
key_facts: Code 7500 indicates unlawful interference
Alerts ATC and military authorities
Continue to nearest suitable aerodrome
Military assistance may be provided
Rare emergency code

syllabus_desc: Normal VFR conspicuity code in NZ
concept: In New Zealand, the standard transponder code for VFR aircraft operating outside controlled airspace is 1200. This code is set by default when the transponder is turned to ALT mode. When operating in controlled airspace or under radar vectors, ATC will assign a specific transponder code. Use mode ALT (altitude reporting) unless specifically instructed otherwise by ATC.
exam_tip: 1200 = Standard NZ VFR code. Use ALT mode to transmit altitude. ATC assigns codes in controlled airspace.
key_facts: Standard NZ VFR code is 1200
Set on ALT mode outside controlled airspace
ATC assigns specific codes in controlled airspace
ALT mode transmits altitude from altimeter
1200 enhances radar conspicuity

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate proficiency in transponder terminology and describe the actions and responses expected from a pilot, following ATC transponder instructions
concept: ATC may instruct pilots to select specific transponder codes, change modes (A, C, S), operate in standby, or squawk ident. Pilots must understand transponder terminology and respond appropriately—selecting the assigned code, confirming readiness, and reporting when the function is complete. In NZ airspace, common codes are 1200 (VFR), 7700 (emergency), 7600 (comms failure), and 7500 (unlawful interference). Pilots must verify code selection and respond professionally to all ATC transponder instructions.
exam_tip: When ATC says 'squawk 2531', set that code. If they say 'squawk ident', press the ident button. Always read back the assigned code to confirm.
key_facts: Standard NZ transponder codes: 1200 (VFR), 7700 (emergency), 7600 (comms failure), 7500 (hijack)
Modes: A (aircraft altitude not available), C (altitude data available), S (with special features)
'Squawk' = select a specific code
'Squawk ident' = press the ident button to enhance return on radar
Always read back assigned codes and confirm mode changes
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25 §2.6.8, AIPNZ ENR 1.1, NZ Civil Aviation Rules Part 91.513

syllabus_desc: List the documents that identify transponder mandatory airspace
concept: Transponder mandatory airspace in New Zealand is defined in the Aeronautical Information Publication NZ (AIPNZ) and the Aeronautical Chart Legend (ACL). The AIPNZ ENR 1.1 section and visual and instrument charts identify controlled airspace where a serviceable transponder (Mode A minimum) is required. Pilots must consult current AIPNZ and relevant charts to identify transponder mandatory areas before flight planning.
exam_tip: Always check AIPNZ ENR 1.1 and current charts for transponder mandatory airspace. It changes—old charts can be misleading.
key_facts: AIPNZ (Aeronautical Information Publication NZ) ENR 1.1 identifies transponder mandatory airspace
Visual Flight Chart (VFC) and Instrument Flight Chart (IFC) mark transponder mandatory areas
Aeronautical Chart Legend (ACL) explains chart symbols for mandatory airspace
Transponder requirements may change—always use current documents
Mode A minimum is standard; Mode C (altitude reporting) increasingly required
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25 §2.6.10, AIPNZ ENR 1.1, NZ Visual Flight Chart

syllabus_desc: Describe the procedures for operations in transponder mandatory airspace when the aircraft transponder is inoperative
concept: If the aircraft transponder becomes inoperative while in or intending to enter transponder mandatory airspace, the pilot must notify ATC immediately and request an alternative clearance or routing outside the mandatory airspace. Operations may continue only with explicit ATC approval and appropriate avoidance of the mandatory zone. If the failure occurs in flight within the mandatory area, the pilot must maintain a listening watch on the assigned frequency, advise ATC of the failure, and await further instructions. Some ATC facilities may grant a special flight permit or reroute the flight to uncontrolled airspace.
exam_tip: Transponder inoperative in mandatory airspace = notify ATC immediately. Don't continue into it unless cleared. Outside = notify anyway before you reach it.
key_facts: Transponder inoperative = notify ATC immediately
Cannot enter transponder mandatory airspace without ATC approval
Request alternative routing or airspace clearance
Maintain listening watch and report position regularly
Some ATC facilities grant special flight permits for inoperative transponders
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25 §2.6.12, AIPNZ ENR 1.1, NZ Civil Aviation Rules Part 91.513

syllabus_desc: ELT purpose and frequencies
concept: The Emergency Locator Transmitter is a radio beacon designed to aid Search and Rescue (SAR) in locating a downed aircraft. When activated (automatically or manually), the ELT transmits a distress signal on 121.5 MHz (aero emergency frequency, continuously monitored) and 406 MHz (satellite-detected frequency). The 121.5 MHz signal is monitored by civil aviation, and the 406 MHz signal reaches international satellite systems (COSPAS-SARSAT), enabling rapid location even in remote areas.
exam_tip: ELT transmits on 121.5 and 406 MHz. Continuously monitored. Activates automatically on impact or manually.
key_facts: ELT aids Search and Rescue location
Primary frequency: 121.5 MHz (aero emergency)
Secondary frequency: 406 MHz (satellite)
121.5 MHz continuously monitored by ATC
406 MHz monitored by COSPAS-SARSAT satellites

syllabus_desc: NZ ELT carriage and registration requirements
concept: Under New Zealand Civil Aviation Rules Part 91.529, all aircraft operating more than 20 nautical miles from an airfield, or above 10,000 feet, must carry an operational ELT. The ELT must be registered with Rescue Coordination Centre New Zealand (RCCNZ). The aircraft registration, owner contact details, and emergency contact information are encoded in the ELT's 406 MHz beacon. Regular maintenance and battery replacement (typically every 6 years or per manufacturer specifications) are mandatory.
exam_tip: CAR 91.529: ELT required for flights >20 nm from airfield or >10,000 ft. Must register with RCCNZ.
key_facts: CAR Part 91.529 mandates ELT carriage
Required for flights >20 nm from airfield
Required for flights above 10,000 feet
ELT must be registered with RCCNZ
Battery replacement required every 6 years

syllabus_desc: Automatic ELT activation on impact
concept: Modern ELTs incorporate a G-switch (accelerometer) that automatically detects sudden deceleration forces typical of an aircraft accident. When deceleration exceeds a threshold (typically 5G), the G-switch triggers automatic activation of the ELT transmitter. This is a critical safety feature because injured pilots may be unable to manually activate the ELT. Automatic activation occurs within seconds of impact, ensuring the distress signal reaches rescuers quickly.
exam_tip: G-switch detects impact (deceleration >5G). Automatically activates ELT. Pilot must then manually turn off after recovery.
key_facts: G-switch triggered by impact deceleration
Automatic activation within seconds of crash
Threshold typically 5G of deceleration
Activates even if pilot is incapacitated
Pilots must manually disable after safe landing

syllabus_desc: Manual ELT activation methods
concept: ELTs can be manually activated in two ways: (1) cockpit remote switch—a small red switch in the cockpit that pilots can activate without leaving the aircraft; (2) direct activation on the ELT unit itself—usually accessed through a small access panel or cover. Manual activation is necessary if the G-switch fails or if the pilot chooses to activate during an in-flight emergency (engine failure, hydraulic loss, fire) before landing. The cockpit switch is preferred as it does not require leaving the cabin.
exam_tip: Manual ELT: cockpit switch (preferred) or direct activation on unit. Use for in-flight emergencies, not just impact.
key_facts: Cockpit remote switch is primary manual method
Direct activation on unit if switch fails
Manual activation for in-flight emergencies
Can be activated before landing if time permits
Some units have small safety covers to prevent accidental activation

syllabus_desc: Post-landing ELT procedures
concept: After a forced landing or accident, if the ELT was activated automatically or manually, the pilot must immediately deactivate it once the aircraft is safely recovered and crew are safe. An active ELT triggers expensive and disruptive Search and Rescue operations. Once safe, transmit on 121.5 MHz or contact ATC/Rescue Coordination Centre New Zealand (RCCNZ) to report the false alarm and allow authorities to cancel the emergency response. Failing to deactivate the ELT after recovery results in wasted SAR resources and potential penalties.
exam_tip: After landing: turn off ELT immediately. Contact ATC/RCCNZ on 121.5 to cancel alert. Report location, crew condition.
key_facts: Deactivate ELT immediately after safe recovery
Contact ATC or RCCNZ on 121.5 MHz
Report aircraft location and crew condition
Provide situation update to cancel SAR response
Failure to deactivate wastes SAR resources

syllabus_desc: ELT testing procedures
concept: ELT testing must be conducted in a controlled manner to avoid triggering false alerts. Testing is restricted to the first 5 minutes of each UTC hour only (not local time). Audio testing (listening to the 121.5 MHz signal) is allowed for up to 3 sweeps of the test tone. Modern 406 MHz ELTs must NOT be tested by activation as this causes the beacon to transmit on satellite frequencies and triggers false alerts. ELT batteries should be checked regularly, and units should be inspected per manufacturer maintenance schedules.
exam_tip: Test in first 5 min of hour only. Max 3 audio sweeps. Never activate 406 MHz beacon—use visual/audio checks only.
key_facts: Testing restricted to first 5 minutes of UTC hour
Audio testing limited to 3 sweeps
Never activate 406 MHz beacon for testing
Battery voltage check recommended
Maintenance per manufacturer schedule required

syllabus_desc: Inadvertent ELT activation and procedures
concept: Occasionally an ELT may be activated inadvertently (by accident during maintenance, during G-switch testing, or due to mechanical failure). If the pilot becomes aware of ELT activation, immediate actions are: (1) confirm activation by monitoring 121.5 MHz for the characteristic ELT audio tone; (2) if activated, deactivate the ELT immediately; (3) contact ATC or RCCNZ on 121.5 MHz to report the false alarm; (4) provide aircraft location, registration, and report that the activation was inadvertent. This prevents costly SAR mobilization.
exam_tip: Inadvertent ELT: listen on 121.5, turn it off immediately, contact ATC/RCCNZ with location and registration.
key_facts: Monitor 121.5 MHz to confirm activation
Deactivate ELT immediately
Contact ATC on 121.5 to report false alarm
Provide aircraft registration and location
Report situation status (crew safe, aircraft secure)

syllabus_desc: Phonetic alphabet in aviation
concept: The phonetic alphabet is used in aviation to avoid confusion when spelling aircraft call signs, waypoint names, or other information over radio. Each letter is represented by a word: ALFA, BRAVO, CHARLIE, DELTA, ECHO, FOXTROT, GOLF, HOTEL, INDIA, JULIETT, KILO, LIMA, MIKE, NOVEMBER, OSCAR, PAPA, QUEBEC, ROMEO, SIERRA, TANGO, UNIFORM, VICTOR, WHISKEY, X-RAY, YANKEE, ZULU. Note the unusual pronunciations: ALFA (not ALPHA), JULIETT (double T), and X-RAY (not XRAY).
exam_tip: Use phonetic alphabet for letters. Notable: ALFA, JULIETT, X-RAY. 26 standard words—learn them all.
key_facts: ALFA, BRAVO, CHARLIE, DELTA, ECHO, FOXTROT, GOLF, HOTEL, INDIA, JULIETT
KILO, LIMA, MIKE, NOVEMBER, OSCAR, PAPA, QUEBEC, ROMEO, SIERRA, TANGO
UNIFORM, VICTOR, WHISKEY, X-RAY, YANKEE, ZULU
Used for call signs, waypoint names, spelling
Avoids confusion between similar-sounding letters

syllabus_desc: Standard number and decimal pronunciation
concept: Numbers in aviation are pronounced in a specific manner to avoid confusion: 0=ZE-RO, 1=WUN, 2=TOO, 3=TREE, 4=FOW-ER, 5=FIFE, 6=SIX, 7=SEV-EN, 8=AIT, 9=NIN-ER. For larger numbers: 100=HUN-DRED, 1000=TOU-SAND. Decimal points are pronounced DAY-SEE-MAL. For example, the altitude 5500 feet is 'FIVE THOUSAND FIVE HUNDRED', or 'FIVE FIFE ZERO ZERO' when phonetically slow. A frequency of 119.15 is 'WUN WUN NIN-ER POINT WUN FIFE' or 'ONE ONE NINER DECIMAL ONE FIVE'.
exam_tip: 0=ZE-RO, 1=WUN, 2=TOO, 3=TREE, 4=FOW-ER, 5=FIFE, 8=AIT, 9=NIN-ER. Decimal=DAY-SEE-MAL.
key_facts: 0 = ZE-RO
1 = WUN
2 = TOO
3 = TREE
4 = FOW-ER
5 = FIFE
6 = SIX
7 = SEV-EN
8 = AIT
9 = NIN-ER
Decimal = DAY-SEE-MAL

syllabus_desc: Aircraft call signs in radio communications
concept: Aircraft identify themselves on radio using their registration or assigned flight plan call sign. For a Cessna with registration ZK-ABC, the call sign is 'ZULU KILO ALPHA BRAVO CHARLIE' or 'ZK ABC' (said as 'Zulu Kilo Alpha Bravo Charlie'). Some aircraft have abbreviated call signs assigned by ATC for efficiency (e.g., 'LION 123'). The aircraft type may be included for recognition purposes (e.g., 'Cessna ZK-ABC'). Registration call signs are preferred for VFR operations and aerodromes without ATC.
exam_tip: Use registration (ZK-ABC = 'Zulu Kilo Alpha Bravo Charlie') or assigned flight plan call sign. Aircraft type optional.
key_facts: Use aircraft registration or flight plan call sign
Registration: ZK-ABC = 'ZK ABC' spelled phonetically
Flight plan call signs are assigned by ATC
Aircraft type may be included for recognition
Consistent use improves ATC situational awareness

syllabus_desc: Standard radio procedures outside controlled airspace
concept: When flying outside controlled airspace (OCTA), pilots must broadcast their position, altitude, and intentions on appropriate frequencies. These frequencies include: the published CTAF for unattended aerodromes, Flight Information Service frequencies (FISCOM) for en-route OCTA operations, Mandatory Broadcast Zone (MBZ) designated frequencies, or Common Frequency Zone (CFZ) frequencies as published in AIPNZ. Broadcasts follow the standard format: 'Auckland Radio, Cessna Zulu Kilo Alpha Bravo Charlie, position [location], altitude [feet], climbing to [altitude], [intentions]'. Regular broadcasts maintain situational awareness for all traffic.
exam_tip: OCTA broadcasts: use published CTAF, FISCOM, MBZ, or CFZ freq. Transmit position, altitude, intentions regularly.
key_facts: Use published CTAF for each aerodrome
FISCOM provides flight information services
MBZ (Mandatory Broadcast Zone) requires specific frequency
CFZ (Common Frequency Zone) operates on designated frequency
Broadcast position, altitude, and intentions regularly

syllabus_desc: Time transmission and UTC format
concept: All aviation communications use Coordinated Universal Time (UTC), also called Zulu time (Z). Time is transmitted in UTC format, with each digit spoken separately. Normally, only minutes are transmitted (e.g., 'Minutes two-zero' = :20), unless confusion is possible, in which case the full time is given (e.g., 'WUN TWO ZULU' = 12:00Z). SARTIME (Search and Rescue Time) includes the full hour for emergency communications. The sun-set position report includes time in UTC. All flight logs, ADS-B, and position reports use UTC.
exam_tip: Use UTC (Zulu). Normally transmit minutes only (e.g., ':20'). Full time (1200Z) if confusion possible.
key_facts: UTC is Coordinated Universal Time (Zulu time)
Normally transmit minutes only
Full hour if confusion likely
SARTIME includes full hour in emergencies
All aviation records use UTC

syllabus_desc: Effective listening techniques
concept: Effective radio listening is critical for flight safety. Pilots should: (1) listen before transmitting to avoid blocking other transmissions; (2) monitor the frequency for 2-3 minutes before making the first call to understand traffic patterns; (3) build a mental picture of other aircraft, their positions, altitudes, and intentions; (4) recognize when frequency congestion is high and break transmissions into shorter messages; (5) listen for position reports and updates from other aircraft to maintain situational awareness. Poor listening discipline causes missed warnings, frequency blocking, and safety incidents.
exam_tip: Listen before transmitting. Monitor for 2-3 min before first call. Build mental traffic picture.
key_facts: Listen before transmitting to avoid blocking
Monitor frequency 2-3 minutes before first transmission
Build mental picture of all traffic
Identify other aircraft positions and altitudes
Improve own situational awareness

syllabus_desc: Four Ws of establishing communication
concept: When establishing radio contact with an ATC facility, Unicom, or Flight Service, use the Four Ws format to clearly identify yourself and your needs: (1) WHO you are calling (station name, e.g., 'Auckland Tower'); (2) WHO you are (aircraft registration or call sign, e.g., 'Cessna ZK-ABC'); (3) WHERE you are (position relative to aerodrome or navigation aid, e.g., '15 miles north of Auckland'); (4) WHAT you want (request for service, e.g., 'request landing clearance'). Example: 'Auckland Tower, Cessna ZK-ABC, 15 miles north, request landing clearance.'
exam_tip: Four Ws: Who (station), Who you are (call sign), Where (position), What (request).
key_facts: Who you're calling: station name
Who you are: aircraft registration/call sign
Where you are: position (distance/direction from aerodrome)
What you want: specific request (landing, traffic info, etc.)
Clear delivery of all four elements improves efficiency

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate proficiency in transmitting and receiving spoken messages — identification of service
concept: When transmitting, always clearly identify which service you are calling (e.g., 'Christchurch Approach', 'Auckland Radio', 'Rangiora Flight Service'). When receiving transmissions, note which service is transmitting so you understand the context and authority of the message. This is especially important in busy airspace where multiple services operate on the same or adjacent frequencies. Clear identification prevents confusion and ensures the correct party is responding.
exam_tip: Always say the service name clearly: 'Christchurch Approach, Cessna 221'. Listen for the service name in responses to confirm you're talking to the right people.
key_facts: Identify the service name when calling (Approach, Tower, Flight Service, Radio)
Services identify themselves in all transmissions
Prevents confusion in busy airspace
Listen carefully to confirm which service is responding
Know the assigned frequency for each service at your destination

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate proficiency in transmitting and receiving spoken messages — radiotelephony aircraft callsigns
concept: The radiotelephony callsign should be your aircraft's registration with phonetic alphabet letters for clarity. For example, ZK-ABC becomes 'Zulu Kilo Able Baker Charlie'. Use the full registration for initial contact, then after establishment, a shortened callsign may be used (e.g., 'Charlie'). Consistent use of correct callsigns prevents confusion with other aircraft and ensures ATC can track your flight accurately. Always be clear and deliberate when spelling out your registration.
exam_tip: Use phonetic alphabet for registration letters: ZK-ABC = 'Zulu Kilo Able Baker Charlie'. After establishment, ATC may clear you to use just 'Charlie'.
key_facts: Full callsign: aircraft registration with phonetic spelling (e.g., ZK-ABC)

Phonetic alphabet: Able, Baker, Charlie, Delta, Echo, Foxtrot, Golf, Hotel, India, Juliet, Kilo, Lima, Mike, November, Oscar, Papa, Quebec, Romeo, Sierra, Tango, Uniform, Victor, Whiskey, X-ray, Yankee, Zulu
Use full callsign for initial contact
Shortened callsign (last letter/number) acceptable after establishment
Always clear and deliberate pronunciation prevents duplication

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate proficiency in transmitting and receiving spoken messages — procedures for exchange of messages
concept: Standard message exchange procedures include: initial callout (callsign + service + position/intent), ATC response, pilot readback of instructions, and confirmation of understanding. Keep transmissions brief and clear. Use standard phraseology to reduce misunderstanding. Always pause before transmitting to ensure the frequency is clear. Wait for a response or confirmation before taking further action. If you don't understand an instruction, ask for clarification immediately rather than guessing.
exam_tip: Listen before you transmit (check frequency is clear). Give callsign, service, position. Wait for response. Read back all clearances. Confirm understanding.
key_facts: Listen before transmitting to ensure frequency is clear
Initial transmission: 'Service name, callsign, position, intention'
Keep transmissions brief and clear
Use standard phraseology
Always read back clearances and instructions
Confirm understanding; ask for clarification if needed

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate proficiency in transmitting and receiving spoken messages — corrections and repetition tests
concept: If you make an error in transmission or receive garbled/unclear instructions, use standard correction phrases. To correct your own transmission, say 'Say again' or 'Correction' and repeat the correct information. If ATC transmits unclearly, respond with 'Say again all after [last clearly understood word]' or 'I require repetition of [specific item]'. For readability checks, use the readability scale (1–5): 1=unreadable, 2=readable with difficulty, 3=readable with some difficulty, 4=readable, 5=perfectly readable. This ensures both parties confirm message reception quality.
exam_tip: Didn't copy that? Say 'Say again all after [last word heard]'. For readability, give a 1–5 scale: '1' is unreadable, '5' is perfect.
key_facts: Use 'Say again' to request repetition of entire message or part
Use 'Say again all after [word]' to repeat from specific point
Readability scale: 1=unreadable, 2=readable now and then, 3=readable with difficulty, 4=readable, 5=perfectly readable
Report readability when asked ('Readability 4')
Always confirm receipt of critical instructions before acting

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate proficiency in transmitting and receiving spoken messages — listening out
concept: Listening out means maintaining a passive listening watch on an assigned frequency without transmitting unless required to report or respond to instructions. Pilots must maintain a continuous listening watch on tower, approach, or flight service frequencies throughout their flight. This allows pilots to hear position reports from other aircraft, weather updates, and any urgent broadcasts. Listening watch is a legal requirement under NZ Civil Aviation Rules Part 91.243 and is critical for maintaining situational awareness and safety.
exam_tip: Keep listening watch even when not transmitting. You need to hear other aircraft, weather updates, and urgent broadcasts. Part 91.243 requires it.
key_facts: Listening watch = passive monitoring of assigned frequency
Required at all times on assigned frequency (Part 91.243)
Allows receipt of position reports, weather, urgent broadcasts
Maintains situational awareness of other traffic
Frequency must be kept clear of interference (squelch properly set)
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25 §2.10.2(l), AIPNZ ENR 1.1, NZ Civil Aviation Rules Part 91.243

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate proficiency in transmitting and receiving spoken messages — readability scale
concept: The readability scale is a standardised 5-point scale for reporting signal quality. It ranges from 1 (unreadable) to 5 (perfectly readable). When ATC or another aircraft asks for a readability report, respond with a number that accurately reflects the quality of the signal you are receiving. For example, 'Readability 4' means the transmission is clear and easily understood. This information helps both parties adjust microphone levels, antenna positioning, or frequency selection if needed.
exam_tip: Readability scale: 1=unreadable, 2=readable now/then, 3=readable with difficulty, 4=readable, 5=perfect. Just give the number: 'Readability 4'.
key_facts: Readability 1: Unreadable
Readability 2: Readable now and then
Readability 3: Readable with difficulty
Readability 4: Readable
Readability 5: Perfectly readable
Report readability when requested: 'Readability 4'

syllabus_desc: NZ Civil Aviation Rules: Radio comms requirements
concept: New Zealand Civil Aviation Rules Part 91 establishes radio communication requirements for all aircraft. CAR 91.217 requires aircraft to carry and operate a functioning VHF radio when flying in controlled airspace. CAR 91.243 mandates two-way radio communication with ATC when operating in Class A/B/C/D airspace. CAR 91.245 requires transponder carriage and operation in controlled airspace above 10,000 feet and above 5,000 feet near major aerodromes. CAR 91.247 requires flight plans for certain operations. CAR 91.249 specifies pilot qualifications (including FRTO certification). CAR 91.513 covers aircraft certification and maintenance. CAR 91.515 requires pre-flight checks. CAR 91.529 mandates ELT carriage for flights >20 nm from aerodrome or >10,000 feet.
exam_tip: Know CAR 91.217 (radio), 91.243 (two-way comms), 91.245 (transponder), 91.529 (ELT), 91.249 (FRTO).
key_facts: CAR 91.217: VHF radio carriage required in controlled airspace
CAR 91.243: Two-way comms required in Class A/B/C/D airspace
CAR 91.245: Transponder required above 10,000 ft in controlled airspace
CAR 91.249: Pilot must hold FRTO certification
CAR 91.529: ELT required for flights >20 nm from aerodrome

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate a good working knowledge of Part 91.217(5)
concept: NZ Civil Aviation Rules Part 91.217(5) specifies radio requirements for flight in controlled airspace. All aircraft operating in controlled airspace must carry a serviceable VHF radio capable of transmitting and receiving on the assigned frequency. The radio must be installed and maintained according to manufacturer specifications. Pilots must ensure the radio is operating before entering controlled airspace and must maintain continuous communication with ATC. Failure to comply is a breach of Part 91 and can result in enforcement action.
exam_tip: Part 91.217(5): Aircraft in controlled airspace MUST have serviceable VHF radio. Check it works before you go in.
key_facts: Part 91.217(5) requires serviceable VHF radio in controlled airspace
Radio must be capable of two-way communication
Radio must be maintained per manufacturer specifications
Continuous communication with ATC is required
Failure to carry/use radio is a breach of Part 91
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25 §2.10.4(a), NZ Civil Aviation Rules Part 91.217(5)

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate a good working knowledge of Part 91.243
concept: NZ Civil Aviation Rules Part 91.243 requires all pilots to maintain a listening watch on the appropriate frequency whenever the aircraft is operating. This applies whether the aircraft is under ATC control, in controlled or uncontrolled airspace, on the ground, or airborne. The listening watch must be maintained on: tower frequency when on the aerodrome; approach/departure frequency when climbing/descending in controlled airspace; flight service frequency in uncontrolled airspace; and 121.5 MHz (emergency frequency) as backup. This is a legal requirement and critical for safety.
exam_tip: Part 91.243: Listen on the assigned frequency at all times. It's a legal requirement and keeps you in the loop.
key_facts: Part 91.243 requires listening watch on assigned frequency
Applies at all times: on ground, airborne, controlled or uncontrolled airspace
Tower frequency when on aerodrome
Approach/departure frequency when climbing/descending
Flight service frequency in uncontrolled airspace
121.5 MHz emergency frequency as backup
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25 §2.10.4(b), NZ Civil Aviation Rules Part 91.243

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate a good working knowledge of Part 91.245(b),(c) and (d)
concept: NZ Civil Aviation Rules Part 91.245(b), (c), and (d) specify rules for use of radio. Subsection (b) requires that radio transmissions be limited to essential communications and kept as brief as possible. Subsection (c) prohibits the testing of radio equipment on an aerodrome without prior permission from air traffic control or aerodrome management. Subsection (d) requires that radio transmissions must comply with standard phraseology and procedures. These rules ensure that airspace remains clear for critical communications and that all pilots follow consistent procedures.
exam_tip: Part 91.245: Keep transmissions brief and essential. Don't test radio on aerodrome without permission. Use standard phraseology.
key_facts: Part 91.245(b): Transmissions must be essential and as brief as possible
Part 91.245(c): Radio testing on aerodrome requires ATC/aerodrome management permission
Part 91.245(d): Transmissions must comply with standard phraseology and procedures
Reduces frequency congestion
Ensures safety through consistent procedures
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25 §2.10.4(c), NZ Civil Aviation Rules Part 91.245(b),(c),(d)

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate a good working knowledge of Part 91.247
concept: NZ Civil Aviation Rules Part 91.247 requires pilots to report their position, altitude, and heading to air traffic services when requested or when prescribed in the flight plan or ATC clearance. Position reports are mandatory in controlled airspace and when flying on a flight plan in uncontrolled airspace. Typical position reports include: aircraft callsign, position (location or distance from a reference point), altitude, heading/track, and any relevant information (weather, equipment serviceability). Accurate position reports are essential for ATC to maintain separation and ensure safety.
exam_tip: Part 91.247: Give position reports when asked or as required in your clearance. Include: callsign, position, altitude, heading.
key_facts: Part 91.247 requires position reports when requested by ATC
Mandatory in controlled airspace
Mandatory on flight plan in uncontrolled airspace
Report includes: callsign, position, altitude, heading, relevant information
Essential for ATC separation and safety
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25 §2.10.4(d), NZ Civil Aviation Rules Part 91.247

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate a good working knowledge of Part 91.249(a) and (b)
concept: NZ Civil Aviation Rules Part 91.249(a) and (b) specify rules for ATC communications. Subsection (a) requires that pilots comply with all instructions issued by ATC, except in an emergency or if compliance would create a safety risk. Pilots must acknowledge all instructions and report back when executed. Subsection (b) requires that if a pilot is unable to comply with an ATC instruction, they must immediately inform ATC and provide the reason (e.g., 'Unable to level at flight level 100, turbulence'). This ensures ATC is aware of limitations and can reassign tasks.
exam_tip: Part 91.249: Follow all ATC instructions unless safety is at risk. If you can't comply, tell ATC immediately why.
key_facts: Part 91.249(a): Comply with all ATC instructions
Exception: if compliance creates safety risk or in emergency
Acknowledge all instructions
Report back when instruction is executed
Part 91.249(b): If unable to comply, inform ATC immediately with reason
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25 §2.10.4(e), NZ Civil Aviation Rules Part 91.249(a),(b)

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate a good working knowledge of Part 91.513
concept: NZ Civil Aviation Rules Part 91.513 specifies rules for use of aircraft transponders. Aircraft must carry and operate a Mode A or Mode C transponder in transponder mandatory airspace as defined in the AIPNZ. The transponder must be set to the assigned code and mode as directed by ATC. The transponder must not be used in standby mode while in controlled airspace unless directed by ATC. Pilots must understand transponder codes, modes, and the ident function. Failure to comply with transponder requirements can result in enforcement action.
exam_tip: Part 91.513: Carry and use transponder in mandatory airspace. Set assigned code. Press ident when asked. Don't use standby in controlled airspace.
key_facts: Part 91.513 requires transponder in mandatory airspace (Mode A minimum)
Set to assigned code as directed by ATC
Don't use standby mode in controlled airspace without ATC permission
Understand transponder modes: A (altitude not available), C (altitude available)
Use ident function to enhance radar return when requested
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25 §2.10.4(f), NZ Civil Aviation Rules Part 91.513

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate a good working knowledge of Part 91.515
concept: NZ Civil Aviation Rules Part 91.515 specifies rules for altitude reporting via Mode C transponder. When operating with Mode C transponder in assigned airspace, the aircraft must report altitude continuously via the transponder. The altitude displayed to ATC must be derived from a calibrated altimeter that meets accuracy standards. Pilots are responsible for ensuring the altimeter is set correctly (QNH for standard pressure) and that the transponder is receiving accurate altitude data. If Mode C altitude reporting is inaccurate or unavailable, pilots must notify ATC immediately.
exam_tip: Part 91.515: Mode C transponder must report accurate altitude. Set your altimeter to QNH. If Mode C fails, tell ATC immediately.
key_facts: Part 91.515 requires continuous altitude reporting via Mode C
Altitude derived from calibrated altimeter
Altimeter must be set to QNH (not flight level)
Transponder must receive accurate altitude data
If Mode C fails or is inaccurate, notify ATC immediately
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25 §2.10.4(g), NZ Civil Aviation Rules Part 91.515

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate a good working knowledge of Part 91.529
concept: NZ Civil Aviation Rules Part 91.529 specifies requirements for Emergency Locator Transmitters (ELTs). All aircraft engaged in operations over water or remote terrain must carry a serviceable ELT. The ELT must be in good working condition, with a current battery, and mounted securely. In an emergency (crash, forced landing), the ELT automatically transmits distress signals on 121.5 MHz (aviation emergency frequency) and 406 MHz (satellite frequency). Pilots must know how to activate ELT manually (if auto-activation fails), test it properly (only during designated test windows), and understand its role in search and rescue operations.
exam_tip: Part 91.529: ELT required for flights over water/remote terrain. Transmits on 121.5 and 406 MHz. Test only in designated windows.
key_facts: Part 91.529 requires ELT in aircraft operating over water or remote terrain
ELT must be serviceable with current battery
Automatic activation upon crash or hard landing
Transmits on 121.5 MHz (aviation) and 406 MHz (satellite)
Manual activation available if auto-activation fails
Test only during designated windows (first 5 mins of hour on 121.5)
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25 §2.10.4(h), NZ Civil Aviation Rules Part 91.529

syllabus_desc: UNICOM: Unattended aerodrome ground communications
concept: UNICOM provides non-certificated air-ground communications at aerodromes without ATC or AFIS (Aerodrome Flight Information Service). In New Zealand, UNICOM operates at aerodromes like Ardmore, Whanganui, and smaller strips. UNICOM is staffed by volunteers or commercial operators (not licensed ATC). Services include: weather information, wind direction/speed, QNH (altimeter setting), aerodrome status, and general information. UNICOM does NOT issue clearances or control traffic—pilots are responsible for separation. Frequency is typically 118.25 MHz or as published in AIPNZ Vol 4.
exam_tip: UNICOM = non-certificated ground comms. No clearances issued. Weather, wind, QNH only. Pilot responsible for separation.
key_facts: UNICOM at unattended aerodromes
Staffed by volunteers or commercial operators
Provides weather, wind, QNH information
Does not issue clearances or control traffic
Pilot responsible for separation decisions

syllabus_desc: ATIS: Aerodrome Terminal Information Service
concept: ATIS is an automated, pre-recorded broadcast of essential aerodrome information at controlled aerodromes. ATIS includes: weather (wind direction/speed, visibility, cloud height, temperature, QNH), active runway(s), any significant information (construction, runway closures, equipment failures, special procedures). ATIS is identified by a phonetic letter (e.g., 'ATIS Alpha', 'ATIS Bravo') which changes every update. Pilots must listen to ATIS before contacting Tower to understand current conditions. The ATIS frequency is published on aerodrome charts. Listening to ATIS before calling Tower reduces frequency congestion and improves coordination.
exam_tip: ATIS = pre-recorded aerodrome info (weather, QNH, runway, conditions). Identified by letter. Listen before Tower call.
key_facts: ATIS is automated pre-recorded broadcast
Includes weather, QNH, runway, significant info
Identified by phonetic letter (Alpha, Bravo, etc.)
Letter changes with each information update
Reduces Tower workload and frequency congestion

syllabus_desc: AFRU: Aerodrome Frequency Response Unit
concept: AFRU is an automated system that acknowledges radio transmissions without human operator involvement. Used at some aerodromes, AFRU provides a radio acknowledgement (e.g., 'Roger') confirming that a transmission was received, but does not provide additional services like weather or separation instructions. AFRU is useful at smaller aerodromes with limited staff. The pilot's responsibility for safe operation remains unchanged—AFRU does not replace ATC or AFIS services.
exam_tip: AFRU = automated acknowledgement system. Confirms transmission received but provides no active service.
key_facts: AFRU provides automated acknowledgement
No human operator involvement
Confirms transmission receipt
Does not provide weather or clearances
Used at aerodromes with limited staffing

syllabus_desc: AWIB: Aerodrome Weather Information Broadcast
concept: AWIB is an automated pre-recorded broadcast of aerodrome and weather information, similar to ATIS but deployed at unattended aerodromes. AWIB provides weather, QNH, wind, and general aerodrome status. The broadcast repeats continuously. AWIB frequency is published on aerodrome charts or in AIPNZ Vol 4. Pilots should listen to AWIB before approaching an unattended aerodrome to obtain current conditions. AWIB may be available on the UNICOM frequency or a dedicated frequency.
exam_tip: AWIB = automated weather/aerodrome info broadcast at unattended strips. Similar to ATIS but not staffed.
key_facts: AWIB is automated pre-recorded broadcast
Used at unattended aerodromes
Provides weather, QNH, wind, status
Continuous repetition
Frequency published on aerodrome charts

syllabus_desc: VFR phraseology for all flight phases
concept: Standard VFR phraseology covers all phases of flight: pre-flight (radio check, flight plan filing); departure (taxi clearance, takeoff, climb-out); en-route (position reports, altitude changes); descent (descent planning, approach notification); landing (downwind, base, final, landing); and post-landing (taxi-in, parking). Phraseology is standardized per AC91-9/AC172-1 to ensure consistent communication. Pilots must practice proper phraseology, correct pacing (not too fast), clear pronunciation, and appropriate brevity. Consistent use of standard phrases reduces confusion and improves safety.
exam_tip: Use standard VFR phraseology per AC91-9. Taxi, takeoff, climb, descent, approach, landing, parking—each has set calls.
key_facts: Standard phraseology per AC91-9/AC172-1
Covers all flight phases
Taxi: request clearance, confirm runway
Takeoff: final check, rolling, airborne
En-route: position reports, altitude updates
Landing: downwind, base, final, touchdown

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate a good working knowledge of the AIPNZ and AC172-1 with regard to communication services
concept: The Aeronautical Information Publication NZ (AIPNZ) ENR 1.1 and Aeronautical Chart Legend (AC172-1) detail all available communication services in New Zealand airspace. These include ATC tower, approach, and departure services at major aerodromes; flight service stations providing weather, NOTAM, and position reports; and UNICOM services at smaller aerodromes. Pilots must understand the purpose of each service, assigned frequencies, operating hours, and how to establish contact. The AIPNZ also lists aerodrome-specific information (runway orientation, lighting, services) essential for flight planning.
exam_tip: AIPNZ ENR 1.1 and AC172-1 are your guides: frequencies, service names, operating hours, services available. Always check current versions.
key_facts: AIPNZ ENR 1.1 lists all communication services in NZ airspace
Tower, Approach, Departure, Flight Service, UNICOM services defined
AC172-1 provides chart symbols and frequencies
Services vary by aerodrome and airspace classification
Operating hours, contact procedures, and services listed for each aerodrome

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate a good working knowledge of the AIPNZ and AC172-1 with regard to communication aspects in the Search and Rescue section
concept: The AIPNZ and AC172-1 contain dedicated sections on Search and Rescue (SAR) communication procedures. This includes: emergency frequencies (121.5 MHz primary, 406 MHz satellite), ELT information, distress and urgency call procedures, and radio silence rules for non-involved stations. Pilots must know how to declare an emergency ('Mayday' for distress, 'Pan Pan' for urgency), what information to provide, and how ATS and other aircraft will respond. The SAR section also outlines the role of aircraft in assisting with search operations and procedures for transmitting blind if unable to establish contact with ATS.
exam_tip: AIPNZ/AC172-1 SAR section: Know 121.5 MHz (emergency), ELT operation, Mayday/Pan Pan procedures, and what to say in emergency.
key_facts: Emergency frequency: 121.5 MHz (aviation), 406 MHz (satellite)
Mayday = distress (life-threatening), Pan Pan = urgency (serious but not life-threatening)
ELT transmits on 121.5 and 406 MHz
All non-involved stations maintain radio silence during emergency
SAR section outlines ATS emergency response and assisting aircraft roles
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25 §2.10.10(b), AIPNZ ENR 1.1 SAR, AC172-1

syllabus_desc: Standard aviation words and phrases
concept: Aviation uses standardized phraseology to eliminate ambiguity. Key terms and meanings: ACKNOWLEDGE = confirm you received message; AFFIRM = yes (not 'affirmative'); APPROVED = clearance given; BREAK = pause in transmission; CANCEL = terminate clearance; CLEARED = authorized for action; CONFIRM = verify correctness; CONTACT = call facility; CORRECT = message is accurate; CORRECTION = disregard previous message; DISREGARD = ignore message; MAINTAIN = continue current altitude/heading/speed; MONITOR = listen to frequency; NEGATIVE = no (not 'negative copy'); READ BACK = repeat back what you heard; ROGER = message received; SAY AGAIN = repeat message; STANDBY = wait; UNABLE = cannot comply; WILCO = will comply. Each term has precise meaning per ICAO Annex 10 and AC91-9/AC172-1.
exam_tip: Learn all standard phrases. Key: AFFIRM (yes), NEGATIVE (no), WILCO (will comply), ROGER (received).
key_facts: ACKNOWLEDGE: confirm receipt
AFFIRM: yes
APPROVED: clearance given
BREAK: pause transmission
CANCEL: terminate
CLEARED: authorized
CONFIRM: verify
CONTACT: call facility
CORRECT: message is accurate
CORRECTION: disregard previous
DISREGARD: ignore
MAINTAIN: continue current
MONITOR: listen
NEGATIVE: no
READ BACK: repeat
ROGER: received
SAY AGAIN: repeat
STANDBY: wait
UNABLE: cannot comply
WILCO: will comply

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate proficiency in standard radiotelephony phraseologies and procedures for all VFR operations in controlled and uncontrolled airspace
concept: VFR operations require pilots to use standardised phraseology for all radio communications. This includes: position reports (location, altitude, heading, flight time to destination), level-offs, altitude changes, turns, and routine communications with ATC. Standard phrases reduce ambiguity and ensure ATC understands pilot intentions clearly. In controlled airspace, pilots must follow ATC clearances and readback procedures strictly. In uncontrolled airspace, pilots maintain listening watch on flight service frequency and report position at designated intervals (typically every 30 minutes or as instructed). Phraseology must be clear, professional, and consistent with AIPNZ and AC172-1 guidelines.
exam_tip: VFR phraseology: Position, altitude, heading, intent. Use standard phrases, read back clearances, maintain listening watch. Same rules apply everywhere.
key_facts: Standard phraseology reduces miscommunication
Position reports: location, altitude, heading, ETA, flight time
Use 'Level' for altitude changes, 'Descending/climbing to' for new altitude
Read back all clearances, changes, and headings
In controlled airspace: follow ATC clearances exactly
In uncontrolled airspace: position reports every 30 minutes on flight service frequency

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate proficiency in standard radiotelephony phraseologies and procedures for taxi, take-off, approach and landing at controlled aerodromes, Flight Service aerodromes, aerodromes served by UNICOM, and uncontrolled aerodromes
concept: Each aerodrome type has specific phraseology and procedures. At controlled aerodromes: request taxi clearance, report ready for takeoff, follow tower instructions for landing. At Flight Service aerodromes: report intentions (landing/overhead), request active runway, report on final. At UNICOM aerodromes: self-announce position on UNICOM frequency (e.g., 'Rangiora UNICOM, Cessna 221 on downwind runway 02'). At uncontrolled aerodromes: self-announce on 122.8 MHz (standard NZ uncontrolled frequency) or aerodrome-specific frequency, giving position and intentions. Standard phraseology must be used throughout all phases to ensure safety and reduce confusion.
exam_tip: Controlled: ask for taxi, takeoff, landing clearances. Flight Service: report position/intentions. UNICOM: self-announce. Uncontrolled: self-announce on 122.8.
key_facts: Controlled aerodromes: request taxi, takeoff, and landing clearance from tower
Flight Service aerodromes: report position (overhead, downwind, final) to flight service
UNICOM aerodromes: self-announce on UNICOM frequency with position and intention
Uncontrolled aerodromes: self-announce on 122.8 MHz (or aerodrome-specific) with position, altitude, intention
Standard phraseology for taxi ('holding point ready for takeoff'), takeoff, approach, landing

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate proficiency in standard radiotelephony phraseologies and procedures for read-back instructions
concept: Read-back is a critical safety procedure where pilots repeat ATC instructions verbatim to confirm understanding and accuracy. All clearances, headings, altitudes, frequencies, and runway assignments must be read back. Example: ATC says 'Climb to flight level 100', pilot responds 'Climbing to flight level 100, Cessna 221'. If the pilot's readback is incorrect, ATC will correct immediately. Read-backs prevent misunderstandings and ensure both parties agree on the instruction before the pilot acts. Pilots must never assume they understand—always read back completely and wait for confirmation.
exam_tip: Read back everything: headings, altitudes, frequencies, runway. Say it back exactly as you heard it. Wait for confirmation before acting.
key_facts: Read back all clearances: altitude, heading, frequency, runway, procedure
Readback must be verbatim from ATC instruction
Pilot: 'Cleared to land runway 02, Cessna 221'
ATC corrects if readback is incorrect
Wait for ATC confirmation before executing instruction
If you don't understand, ask for clarification instead of guessing

syllabus_desc: Prohibited unauthorized transmissions
concept: CAA rules prohibit: (1) transmitting on aviation frequencies without proper authority or license (FRTO certification required); (2) making unnecessary transmissions that clog the frequency; (3) transmitting misleading signals or false information that could confuse ATC or other pilots; (4) keying the microphone without intent to communicate (accidentally blocking frequency). Pilots must respect frequency discipline—only transmit when necessary, speak clearly and briefly, and never joke or transmit false information. Violations can result in license suspension or prosecution.
exam_tip: Never transmit without authority. No unnecessary calls. No misleading or false info. No frequency blocking.
key_facts: FRTO certification required to transmit
Unauthorized transmissions are prohibited
No unnecessary calls
No misleading or false information
No deliberate frequency blocking
source: NZ Civil Aviation Rules, AC91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16

syllabus_desc: Secrecy of communications
concept: All radio communications in aviation (whether intercepted, overheard, or received during flight) must be treated as confidential. Pilots, ground staff, and ATC must not disclose the contents of communications without proper authorization. This protects: (1) security of flight operations; (2) privacy of crews and passengers; (3) confidential operational and emergency information. Improper disclosure of communications could compromise security or assist adversaries. Secrecy is a legal obligation under aviation regulations and must be respected.
exam_tip: All comms are confidential. Don't disclose what you hear. Applies to ATC, crews, ground staff, everyone.
key_facts: All intercepted communications are confidential
Must not be disclosed without authorization
Protects security of operations
Protects crew and passenger privacy
Legal obligation under aviation regulations
source: NZ Civil Aviation Rules, AC91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16

syllabus_desc: Controlled aerodrome phraseology
concept: At controlled aerodromes (with ATC Tower), phraseology follows strict procedures: (1) Initial contact includes ATIS information reception, call sign, position, altitude, intentions; (2) Tower acknowledges and provides clearance (routing, initial altitude); (3) Read back all clearances; (4) Circuit calls include downwind (confirm height, heading), base (confirm descent), final (confirm landing); (5) Tower clears for landing, land, and taxi in; (6) After touchdown, taxi to parking following Tower instructions. All readbacks must confirm: altitude, heading, speed, frequency, transponder code.
exam_tip: Tower sequence: ATIS listen, initial contact (with ATIS letter), acknowledge/readback, circuit calls, landing, taxi-in.
key_facts: Listen to ATIS before Tower call
Initial contact: call sign, position, altitude, intentions
Read back all clearances
Circuit calls: downwind, base, final
Confirm: altitude, heading, speed, frequency

syllabus_desc: AFIS aerodrome phraseology
concept: At AFIS aerodromes (Flight Service, but not ATC), procedures differ from controlled aerodromes. AFIS does NOT issue clearances—it provides information (weather, QNH, traffic, runway in use). Pilots are responsible for separation decisions. Phraseology: (1) initial contact with AFIS (position, altitude, intentions); (2) AFIS provides information (similar to ATIS) and reports any traffic; (3) pilot makes separation decisions based on information provided; (4) pilot reports downwind, base, final independently; (5) AFIS acknowledges but does not 'clear' for landing—landing is pilot's decision. This places greater responsibility on the pilot.
exam_tip: AFIS provides INFO, not clearances. Pilot responsible for separation. Report position (downwind, base, final) for traffic awareness.
key_facts: AFIS provides information (weather, traffic)
Does not issue clearances
Pilot responsible for separation decisions
Pilot reports circuit positions independently
Landing is pilot's decision, not AFIS decision

syllabus_desc: Unattended aerodrome phraseology
concept: At unattended aerodromes (no ATC or AFIS), pilots broadcast on the published CTAF (Common Traffic Advisory Frequency) to alert other aircraft. Phraseology uses 'TRAFFIC' not the station name (since no station exists). Calls for: taxi ('Cessna ZK-ABC, taxiing runway 06'); rolling ('ZK-ABC, rolling runway 06'); circuit positions using published reporting points ('ZK-ABC, downwind [reporting point], 1000 feet'); landing ('ZK-ABC, final runway 06'); landed ('ZK-ABC, landed runway 06'). Broadcasts maintain 2-3 minute separation information and allow other aircraft to maintain situational awareness. Pilots are fully responsible for separation.
exam_tip: Unattended = broadcast 'TRAFFIC' not station name. Use published CTAF. Report taxi, rolling, circuit, landing.
key_facts: Use 'TRAFFIC' prefix, not station name
Use the published CTAF for the aerodrome
Broadcast: taxi, rolling, circuit positions, landing
Include reporting points for position clarity
Pilot responsible for separation

syllabus_desc: MBZ (Mandatory Broadcast Zone) procedures
concept: Some aerodromes have Mandatory Broadcast Zones (MBZ) where all aircraft must transmit position reports on a designated frequency. MBZ details are published in AIPNZ Vol 4. Pilots must use the specific MBZ frequency (not the CTAF or other frequencies), report position at prescribed intervals (typically 10 nm, 5 nm, and 2 nm before the aerodrome, plus initial contact), include altitude, intentions, and estimate. MBZ reports help ensure traffic separation and aerodrome coordination even without active ATC. Non-compliance with MBZ requirements may result in penalties or denial of landing clearance.
exam_tip: MBZ uses specific frequency (published in AIPNZ). Report at 10 nm, 5 nm, 2 nm, plus initial contact.
key_facts: MBZ frequency published in AIPNZ Vol 4
Must use designated MBZ frequency (not the CTAF)
Report at prescribed intervals (10 nm, 5 nm, 2 nm)
Include altitude, intentions, estimate
Non-compliance may result in penalties

syllabus_desc: Readback requirements for ATC clearances
concept: Pilots must read back all ATC clearances to confirm correct understanding. Readback covers: (1) route clearance (airways, waypoints); (2) approach/departure clearance (vectoring, altitude); (3) conditional clearances (e.g., 'cleared to land, wind 270/15'); (4) runway clearance (e.g., 'cleared to land runway 24L'); (5) transponder codes; (6) level/heading/speed instructions; (7) altimeter settings (QNH); (8) frequency changes. Non-readback items (informational only) can be acknowledged with 'ROGER' or 'WILCO' without reading back. Readback must be immediate, exact, and include the aircraft call sign. Readback errors allow ATC to correct before issuing final clearance.
exam_tip: Read back: routes, clearances, codes, levels, headings, speeds, QNH, frequencies. Use WILCO for non-readback items.
key_facts: Read back route clearances exactly
Read back approach/departure clearances
Read back runway clearance
Read back transponder codes
Read back level, heading, speed instructions
Read back altimeter setting (QNH)
Read back frequency changes
WILCO for non-readback items (info only)

syllabus_desc: MAYDAY distress calls
concept: MAYDAY is used when an aircraft is in grave and imminent danger and requires immediate assistance. The word 'MAYDAY' should be said three times to ensure recognition over static or noise. MAYDAY is transmitted on the current frequency if ATC is present, or on 121.5 MHz (the international aero emergency frequency) if no ATC. Once MAYDAY is transmitted, all other stations must cease transmissions and listen. The aircraft in distress has absolute priority. MAYDAY must be accompanied by: call sign, nature of emergency, present position, altitude, heading, souls on board, endurance, and any other relevant information.
exam_tip: MAYDAY MAYDAY MAYDAY = grave and imminent danger. Transmit 3 times. All other stations silence and listen.
key_facts: MAYDAY = general emergency requiring immediate help
Said three times
Transmit on current frequency or 121.5 MHz
All other stations maintain silence
Highest priority handled by ATC/SAR

syllabus_desc: PAN PAN urgency calls
concept: PAN PAN is used when an aircraft has an urgent situation but does not require immediate assistance. Examples: engine overheating, persistent electrical failure, persistent radio interference, loss of an engine, structural damage, but aircraft is controllable and capable of reaching an aerodrome. PAN PAN is said three times. Transmit on the current frequency or 121.5 MHz. All other stations should maintain silence until the situation is resolved or no longer urgent. PAN PAN situations still receive priority handling but less urgent than MAYDAY. Aircraft in PAN PAN situations will be vectored to the nearest suitable landing place, but may have slightly longer to land compared to MAYDAY situations.
exam_tip: PAN PAN (said 3 times) = urgent but not grave danger. Less priority than MAYDAY but priority over routine traffic.
key_facts: PAN PAN = urgent situation needing priority handling
Not requiring immediate assistance
Said three times
Transmit on current frequency or 121.5 MHz
Other stations maintain silence

syllabus_desc: Emergency frequencies for MAYDAY/PAN PAN
concept: When a MAYDAY or PAN PAN is declared, the pilot should transmit on: (1) the current ATC frequency if in contact with ATC; (2) 121.5 MHz if outside ATC coverage or radio failure. The 121.5 MHz emergency frequency is continuously monitored by ATC facilities, AFIS, Flight Information Service, and many aircraft. Using the current frequency allows ATC to provide immediate vectors and separation services. Using 121.5 MHz ensures detection if the current frequency is not monitored. Modern aircraft with satellite/datalink may also transmit emergency messages via satellite systems.
exam_tip: MAYDAY/PAN PAN: First try current frequency (ATC is listening). Then 121.5 MHz if no response.
key_facts: Current ATC frequency if in contact
121.5 MHz (international emergency frequency)
Both frequencies continuously monitored
Current frequency preferred if ATC available
121.5 MHz if outside ATC coverage

syllabus_desc: Stations to call in emergency
concept: When declaring MAYDAY or PAN PAN, address the call to: (1) the current ATC station (e.g., 'Auckland Tower'); (2) if no response, 'ALL STATIONS' followed by the emergency message; (3) Flight Information Service if no ATC available; (4) Any aircraft monitoring the frequency (by calling 'ALL STATIONS'). Example: 'ALL STATIONS, ALL STATIONS, MAYDAY, Cessna ZK-ABC, engine failure, position...' The first station to hear becomes the primary coordinator and ensures SAR is activated. Multiple stations hearing the call will relay to SAR as needed.
exam_tip: Address to ATC first. If no response, call 'ALL STATIONS'. Whichever station responds first takes action.
key_facts: Address to current ATC station if in contact
Call 'ALL STATIONS' if no ATC response
First responder coordinates SAR activation
Multiple stations relay if needed
Station of responsibility determined by frequency

syllabus_desc: MAYDAY message contents
concept: A complete MAYDAY message includes: (1) 'MAYDAY' (said three times); (2) station addressed (e.g., 'Auckland Tower'); (3) aircraft call sign (repeated twice); (4) nature of emergency (e.g., 'engine failure', 'fire', 'hydraulic loss'); (5) intention (e.g., 'attempting to land', 'ditching in sea', 'staying airborne'); (6) present position (latitude/longitude or distance/direction from navigation aid); (7) flight level/altitude (e.g., '2000 feet'); (8) heading (e.g., '180 degrees'); (9) souls on board (crew + passengers); (10) endurance (fuel time remaining); (11) any other useful information (injuries, structural damage). Example: 'MAYDAY MAYDAY MAYDAY, Auckland Tower, Cessna ZK-ABC ZK-ABC, engine failure, attempting forced landing, 15 nm south of Auckland, 2000 feet, heading 350, 2 souls on board, 20 minutes fuel.'
exam_tip: MAYDAY contents: nature, intention, position, altitude, heading, souls, endurance, other relevant info.
key_facts: MAYDAY (3 times)
Station addressed
Call sign (2x)
Nature of emergency
Intention (land, ditch, etc.)
Position (lat/lon or distance/direction)
Altitude/flight level
Heading
Souls on board
Fuel endurance remaining

syllabus_desc: Radio silence in distress situations
concept: Once a MAYDAY or PAN PAN is acknowledged by ATC or another station, all other aircraft must maintain RADIO SILENCE on that frequency. This means: (1) no other transmissions except to acknowledge you have heard the distress call; (2) keep the frequency clear for the aircraft in distress and the coordinating station; (3) listen and monitor the frequency; (4) provide assistance only if requested or if you can render aid. Radio silence is terminated by the coordinating station with 'DISTRESS TRAFFIC ENDED' (for MAYDAY) or the equivalent for PAN PAN. This discipline is critical—violations can interfere with lifesaving communications.
exam_tip: MAYDAY/PAN PAN acknowledged = radio silence for all others. Listen, don't transmit. Wait for 'DISTRESS TRAFFIC ENDED'.
key_facts: All stations maintain silence except distress station
No unnecessary transmissions
Keep frequency clear
Listen and monitor only
Provide help only if requested
Silence ends with 'DISTRESS TRAFFIC ENDED'

syllabus_desc: Describe the actions by stations not involved in the emergency with regard to radio silence
concept: When an aircraft declares distress (Mayday) or urgency (Pan Pan), all other stations on the frequency must maintain radio silence to clear the frequency for emergency communications. This means ceasing all non-emergency transmissions immediately. Only ATC, rescue services, and the emergency aircraft may transmit. Even if you have important information to communicate, you must remain silent unless directly requested by ATC or the emergency aircraft. Radio silence is critical because overlapping transmissions could mask the emergency signal or distract ATC from handling the crisis. Silence continues until ATC declares the emergency terminated.
exam_tip: Hear Mayday or Pan Pan? Stop all transmissions immediately. Radio silence until ATC says otherwise. Don't interrupt the emergency.
key_facts: All non-involved stations must maintain radio silence during emergency
No non-emergency transmissions allowed
Only ATC, rescue services, emergency aircraft transmit
Radio silence is legally required
Prevents interference with emergency signal
Silence continues until ATC declares emergency terminated
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25 §2.14.6(a), AIPNZ ENR 1.1, ICAO Annex 10

syllabus_desc: Describe the actions by stations not involved in the emergency with regard to provision of assistance
concept: While maintaining radio silence, non-involved aircraft and stations should position themselves to provide assistance if needed. This may include: maintaining listening watch and readiness to relay messages if the emergency aircraft cannot contact ATC directly, noting the aircraft's position and direction of travel, preparing to provide position reports or other information if requested, and if equipped, monitoring frequencies 121.5 MHz and 406 MHz (ELT frequencies). If you are the closest aircraft to the emergency, you may be tasked by ATC to assist with position reports, descent monitoring, or other support. Assistance is provided silently unless explicitly requested by ATC or the emergency aircraft.
exam_tip: Hear an emergency? Stay silent but stay ready. Listen for ATC to ask you for help (relay, position reports, etc.). Only transmit if asked.
key_facts: Non-involved aircraft remain ready to assist if tasked
May relay messages if emergency aircraft cannot reach ATC
May provide position reports or descent monitoring if asked
Monitor 121.5 MHz and 406 MHz (ELT frequencies) if equipped
Only transmit assistance if requested by ATC or emergency aircraft
Assistance provided silently unless explicit transmission requested
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25 §2.14.6(b), AIPNZ ENR 1.1 SAR, ICAO Annex 10

syllabus_desc: Describe the procedure involved in terminating emergency communications
concept: Emergency communications are terminated by ATC only, never by the pilot. Once the emergency is resolved (aircraft lands safely, crisis resolved, or situation no longer life-threatening), ATC will explicitly state 'Emergency terminated' or similar. At that point, radio silence is lifted, normal communications resume, and other aircraft may resume regular transmissions. The emergency aircraft may be assigned a departure frequency or normal operating frequency. A full incident report will typically be filed by ATC and the pilot. Pilots should not declare the emergency over unilaterally; always wait for ATC to confirm termination.
exam_tip: Only ATC can end the emergency. Wait for ATC to say 'Emergency terminated' before you consider it over.
key_facts: Only ATC can declare emergency terminated
Pilot does not unilaterally end emergency
ATC will state explicitly: 'Emergency terminated' or similar
Radio silence lifted only after ATC declaration
Emergency aircraft assigned normal frequency after termination
Incident report filed by ATC and pilot
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25 §2.14.8, AIPNZ ENR 1.1 SAR, ICAO Annex 10

syllabus_desc: Troubleshooting radio failure
concept: If radio communication is lost, immediately troubleshoot: (1) Confirm PTT released (you're not holding it down); (2) Check squelch is set correctly (not too high—weak signals may not open squelch); (3) Verify radio/intercom selector is in correct position (COM 1, 2, or AUX); (4) Confirm correct radio is selected (active, not standby); (5) Check volume is audible; (6) Verify correct frequency is dialed on active radio; (7) Ensure headset jacks fully inserted in receptacles; (8) Check master and avionics switches are ON (battery power); (9) Inspect fuses and circuit breakers for failures; (10) Try spare headset if available; (11) Rotate aircraft 90° to change antenna attitude (terrain may block); (12) Climb higher if possible (expands radio horizon); (13) Try alternate radio if available. Systematically work through these checks.
exam_tip: Troubleshoot: PTT release, squelch, selector, frequency, volume, jacks, master/avionics, fuses, spare headset, antenna position.
key_facts: Check PTT is released
Verify squelch setting
Confirm correct selector position
Check correct radio selected
Verify volume is audible
Check frequency dialed correctly
Ensure headset jacks fully inserted
Check master/avionics switches ON
Inspect fuses/circuit breakers
Try spare headset
Rotate aircraft to change antenna
Climb higher if possible
Try alternate radio

syllabus_desc: Describe the checks when becoming aware of aircraft communication system failure — radio power source
concept: When you suspect communication system failure, systematically check the power source first. Verify that the aircraft's electrical system is functioning (check ammeter, vacuum/pressure indicator, and other electrical instruments). Ensure the radio master switch is in the ON position and that the radio power is not inadvertently switched off. Check that the battery is supplying power (ammeter shows charging). If electrical power is lost, the radio will not function. In GA aircraft, a dual-battery system or electrical backup may be available; check and switch if needed. Power source failure is the most common cause of radio loss and should be ruled out first.
exam_tip: Radio died? First check: Is the aircraft electrical system working? Master switch ON? Battery charging? Fix power, you fix radio.
key_facts: Check aircraft electrical system status (ammeter, vacuum indicator)
Verify radio master switch is ON
Confirm battery is charging (ammeter shows positive charging)
Check for blown circuit breakers (if accessible)
Verify radio power switch is ON (separate from master)
If dual battery system, attempt to switch batteries

syllabus_desc: Describe the checks when becoming aware of aircraft communication system failure — function settings (frequency, squelch and similar)
concept: After confirming electrical power, check all radio function settings. Verify the frequency is correctly set and matches the intended frequency (cross-check against chart/NOTAM). Confirm the volume is turned up (not muted) and the squelch setting is appropriate (too high = no weak signals; too low = excessive static). Check that the radio mode is set correctly (VHF, not HF or other mode if dual radio). Ensure the PTT (push-to-talk) button is functioning and not stuck. On some radios, a standby/active switch may be set wrong; verify the active frequency is correct. Incorrect settings are common causes of apparent communication failure.
exam_tip: Frequency set right? Volume up? Squelch reasonable? PTT button working? Check all settings before you decide the radio is broken.
key_facts: Verify frequency matches intended frequency (cross-check chart)
