Check volume is turned UP (not muted)
Squelch set appropriately (not too high, not too low)
Confirm radio mode is correct (VHF, not HF)
Test PTT button for responsiveness (not stuck)
If dual radio, verify correct radio/frequency is selected

syllabus_desc: Describe the checks when becoming aware of aircraft communication system failure — microphone or headset
concept: If power and frequency settings are correct, the microphone or headset may be faulty. Check the microphone connector for loose connections or damage. Ensure the headset is seated firmly in the jack. Test headset function: you should hear static or audio when the volume is turned up. If the boom microphone is disconnected or damaged, attempt to use the handheld microphone (if available). Check the PTT button on the microphone for mechanical problems (not depressed, stuck, or damaged). Swap microphones or headsets if spares are available. A loose microphone cable or damaged headset speaker is a common cause of apparent total radio failure.
exam_tip: Mic or headset problem? Check connections—both should be plugged in firmly. Try spare mic/headset if available. Or use handheld mic.
key_facts: Verify microphone connector is firmly seated and undamaged
Check headset connection (should be snug)
Test headset audio function (should hear static or audio at volume)
Inspect boom microphone for damage or disconnection
Test PTT button for mechanical problems
Swap with spare microphone or headset if available
Use handheld microphone as backup

syllabus_desc: Actions on loss of communications (comms failure)
concept: If radio failure is confirmed (cannot transmit or receive after troubleshooting), take these actions: (1) Set transponder 7600 immediately to alert ATC to radio failure; (2) Maintain current altitude and heading (ATC may vector via radar); (3) Try secondary ATS frequencies if available (HF, VHF backup); (4) Monitor ATIS to understand current conditions and runway in use; (5) Transmit 'blind' on current frequency (prefix 'TRANSMITTING BLIND') stating position and intentions—ATC may be able to hear you; (6) Try mobile phone to contact ATC if available; (7) Maintain terrain clearance and stay in visual flight conditions; (8) If VFR: divert to unattended aerodrome and land; (9) If unable to land at intended destination: use 'speechless technique' per AIPNZ ENR 1.15 (squawk 7600, follow radar vectors via turns). NZ CAR 91.217 requires radio carriage; flying with failed radio is a violation.
exam_tip: Comms failure: 7600 on transponder, maintain altitude/heading, monitor ATIS, transmit blind, try mobile phone, land at unattended field.
key_facts: Set transponder 7600
Maintain altitude and heading
Try secondary frequencies (HF, backup VHF)
Monitor ATIS for conditions
Transmit blind if possible
Try mobile phone to ATC
Maintain terrain clearance
Divert to unattended field if VFR
Use speechless technique if possible
Follow radar vectors (ATC may vector via transponder)

syllabus_desc: Detail the actions to be taken when experiencing loss of communications in controlled airspace
concept: Loss of radio communication in controlled airspace is serious. First, try all troubleshooting steps listed in 2.16.2 (power, frequency, settings, microphone). If communication is restored, report the loss to ATC immediately. If communication cannot be restored: Squawk 7600 (communications failure code) on the transponder to alert ATC. Attempt to establish contact on alternate frequencies (e.g., 121.5 MHz emergency frequency, or nearby ATC frequencies). If equipped, try second radio. Listen on all frequencies for ATC instructions via light signals or other guidance. Follow the aircraft's last assigned clearance, altitude, and heading. File a position report in the blind (transmit without expectation of receipt). Descend to circuit altitude and attempt landing at the nearest suitable aerodrome. Land only when safe to do so.
exam_tip: Lost comms in controlled airspace? Squawk 7600, try 121.5, listen for light signals. Follow last clearance/altitude. Land at nearest safe aerodrome.
key_facts: Troubleshoot: power, frequency, settings, microphone first
Squawk 7600 on transponder
Try 121.5 MHz (emergency frequency)
Try alternate frequencies or second radio
Transmit in the blind ('Christchurch Approach, Cessna 221, comms failure')
Follow last assigned clearance, altitude, heading
Listen for ATC light signals
Descend to circuit altitude and land at nearest suitable aerodrome
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25 §2.16.4(a), AIPNZ ENR 1.1 SAR, NZ Civil Aviation Rules Part 91

syllabus_desc: Detail the actions to be taken when experiencing loss of communications in uncontrolled airspace
concept: Loss of communication in uncontrolled airspace is less critical but still requires action. First, troubleshoot: check power, frequency, settings, microphone. Try to establish contact on the flight service frequency or 121.5 MHz. If you have second radio, attempt on alternate frequency. Transmit position reports in the blind (without expectation of receipt) at regular intervals. If equipped with transponder, squawk 1200 (standard VFR code). Continue flight, maintaining visual lookout for other traffic. Proceed to your destination or nearest suitable aerodrome. Upon landing, file a report with the aerodrome operator or flight service explaining the communication loss. Continue visual flight if conditions permit; do not attempt instrument flight without communication.
exam_tip: Lost comms in uncontrolled airspace? Try flight service frequency, 121.5, second radio. Transmit in the blind. Keep flying, land at nearest aerodrome. Report loss.
key_facts: Troubleshoot: power, frequency, settings, microphone first
Try flight service frequency or 121.5 MHz
Try second radio if available
Squawk 1200 (VFR code)
Transmit in the blind at regular intervals
Maintain visual lookout and flight conditions
Continue to destination or nearest suitable aerodrome
File report upon landing
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25 §2.16.4(b), AIPNZ ENR 1.1, NZ Civil Aviation Rules Part 91

syllabus_desc: Detail the actions to be taken when experiencing loss of communications in special use airspace
concept: Special use airspace (e.g., restricted areas, danger areas, training areas) has specific communication requirements and procedures. Loss of communication in special use airspace requires immediate action to avoid violations or collisions. First, troubleshoot as in 2.16.2. If communication cannot be restored, immediately exit the special use airspace if safely possible. Squawk 7600 on transponder. Try 121.5 MHz and any alternate frequencies. Transmit in the blind informing any ATS facility or flight service of communication loss and your position. Comply with any light signals from ATC. If operating under special clearance or for specific operations (e.g., military training), notify the appropriate controlling authority by landing and reporting the loss. Do not continue operations in special use airspace without established communication.
exam_tip: Lost comms in special use airspace? Exit immediately if safe. Squawk 7600. Try 121.5. Transmit in the blind. Report to controlling authority.
key_facts: Special use airspace has strict communication requirements
Loss of comms = immediate action required
Exit airspace if safely possible
Squawk 7600
Try 121.5 MHz and alternate frequencies
Transmit in the blind
Listen for light signals
Report to controlling authority upon landing
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25 §2.16.4(c), AIPNZ ENR 1.1, NZ Civil Aviation Rules Part 91

syllabus_desc: Describe corrective actions that could be taken, including change of frequency or station
concept: If communication is lost or severely degraded, attempt to contact a different ATS facility on an alternate frequency. In New Zealand, major centres have multiple frequencies: Christchurch Approach/Tower use different frequencies; Flight Service provides coverage in uncontrolled airspace. Attempt to switch to the flight service frequency if you lose contact with approach. If you hear other aircraft on a frequency, switch to that frequency and report your communication problem. Try 121.5 MHz (emergency frequency) as a last resort. Changing frequency may restore contact with an adjacent facility and establish communication. Document the frequencies you try and times for later incident reporting.
exam_tip: Lost contact? Try another ATS frequency for the same service, or flight service. If that fails, try 121.5. Document what you tried.
key_facts: Switch to alternate ATC frequency for same service (e.g., Approach 1 to Approach 2)
Try Flight Service frequency if lost contact with Approach
Try 121.5 MHz (emergency frequency)
Listen for other aircraft on frequencies; may be able to relay
Document all frequencies attempted and times
May restore contact with adjacent ATS facility
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25 §2.16.6(a), AIPNZ ENR 1.1, NZ Civil Aviation Rules Part 91

syllabus_desc: Describe corrective actions that could be taken, including transmitting blind
concept: Transmitting in the blind means sending radio messages without expectation of receiving a response from ATC. If you have lost receiving capability but can still transmit, or if you believe ATC cannot hear you, transmit your position report, intentions, and request on all available frequencies (approach frequency, flight service frequency, 121.5 MHz). Speak clearly and slowly. Provide your aircraft callsign, position, altitude, heading, condition (comms failure), and intentions. Other aircraft may relay your message to ATC. ATS may respond via light signals (from chase aircraft or from ground), or may clear other traffic away from your position. Transmitting in the blind is critical for situational awareness and ATS safety even without confirmation of receipt.
exam_tip: Transmitting blind: Say callsign, position, altitude, heading, 'communications failure', and intentions. Speak slowly and clearly. Try all frequencies.
key_facts: Transmitting blind = no expectation of receipt
Include: callsign, position, altitude, heading, 'communications failure', intentions
Speak clearly and slowly
Transmit on approach, flight service, and 121.5 frequencies
Other aircraft may relay message
ATS may respond via light signals or by clearing traffic
Enhances situational awareness even without confirmation
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25 §2.16.6(b), AIPNZ ENR 1.1 SAR, NZ Civil Aviation Rules Part 91

syllabus_desc: Describe corrective actions that could be taken, including increase in altitude
concept: Radio range is limited by line-of-sight; higher altitude extends communication range. If you lose radio contact or signals are weak, climb to increase altitude (within safe limits and current clearance). This raises you above terrain obstacles and extends the radio horizon, potentially restoring contact with distant ATS facilities. For example, climbing from 1,000 ft to 3,000 ft can dramatically improve range. If you have lost contact with Approach, climbing may extend your range to a Flight Service station on high ground or to a distant Approach facility. Altitude increase is particularly helpful in mountainous terrain where valleys block radio propagation. Always ensure altitude increase is safe and complies with your current clearance or visual flight rules.
exam_tip: Weak radio or lost contact? Climb higher—better range. You'll see the horizon further, radio will reach further. Use safe altitude limits.
key_facts: Higher altitude = greater radio range (line of sight improves)
Climb to increase communication horizon
Typical rule: range (nm) ≈ 1.23 × √(altitude in feet)
Particularly effective in mountainous terrain
Always ensure altitude increase is safe
Comply with current clearance or VFR altitude limits
May restore contact with distant ATS facility
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25 §2.16.6(c), AIPNZ ENR 1.1, NZ Civil Aviation Rules Part 91

syllabus_desc: Detail the speechless technique using unmodulated transmissions
concept: The speechless (or 'no-modulation') technique is used when you can transmit but cannot receive, or when you can only transmit unmodulated signals (carrier wave only, no voice). The pilot transmits using the PTT button in a series of short and long transmissions to acknowledge ATC instructions or answer yes/no questions. Typically: one short transmission = 'No' or 'I do not understand'; two short transmissions = 'Yes' or 'I understand'. ATC will provide instructions or ask questions in a format that can be answered with this pattern (e.g., 'Is heading 180 acceptable? Two presses if yes'). This technique allows critical communication to continue even when voice transmission is impossible. It is rarely used but essential knowledge for emergency situations.
exam_tip: Speechless technique: One key press = No. Two key presses = Yes. ATC will ask yes/no questions. Follow their instructions for the pattern.
key_facts: Speechless technique = no voice transmission, only PTT key
One key press (short transmission) = 'No'
Two key presses (two short transmissions) = 'Yes'
ATC asks yes/no questions; you answer with key presses
Example: 'Descend to 2,000 feet acceptable? Two presses if yes'
Rarely used but critical for total voice failure
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25 §2.16.8, AIPNZ ENR 1.1 SAR, ICAO Annex 10

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of light signals used by ATC
concept: When radio communication is lost, ATC may use light signals from a follow-me aircraft, chase aircraft, or from ground control to provide instructions. NZ light signals are standardised: Steady green = 'You are cleared to land' (from ground); Steady red = 'Do not land, give way to other aircraft, go around' (from ground); Green flashes = 'You are cleared to taxi' (on ground); Red flashes = 'Aerodrome unsafe, do not land'; White flashes = 'Return to starting point' (aerodrome). Pilots must know these signals and be prepared to respond with their own light signals (wing rocking, aircraft light flashing) to acknowledge understanding. Light signals are critical fallback communication when radio fails.
exam_tip: NZ light signals: Steady green = land. Steady red = go around. Green flashes = taxi. Red flashes = don't land. White flashes = return to start.
key_facts: Steady green light (from ground) = cleared to land
Steady red light (from ground) = do not land, give way, go around
Green flashes (from ground) = cleared to taxi
Red flashes (from ground or air) = aerodrome unsafe, do not land
White flashes (from ground or air) = return to starting point
Acknowledge light signals with wing rock or own aircraft light flashing
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25 §2.16.10, AIPNZ ENR 1.1, NZ Visual Flight Chart

syllabus_desc: Traffic Information Broadcasts by Aircraft (TIBA)
concept: TIBA (Traffic Information Broadcasts by Aircraft) is an ATS contingency procedure introduced when normal ATS communications are disrupted. Aircraft broadcast their position, altitude, heading, and intentions on a designated frequency (usually the last known frequency or a published contingency frequency). The broadcast includes position relative to navigation aids, type of operation (VFR/IFR), and estimated next position. TIBA allows aircraft to maintain situational awareness and coordinate with each other and any monitoring stations even when normal ATS is unavailable. Per AIPNZ ENR 1.15, pilots must understand and be able to execute TIBA procedures. TIBA is not normal operations—it is a fallback when ATS systems fail.
exam_tip: TIBA = contingency procedure when ATS fails. Aircraft broadcast position, altitude, intentions on designated frequency.
key_facts: TIBA is ATS contingency procedure
Used when normal ATS communications fail
Aircraft broadcast own position/altitude/intentions
Maintains situational awareness
Coordinates traffic without ATC
Per AIPNZ ENR 1.15

topic: 2.18 — Loss of Communications — ATS Equipment Failure
syllabus_desc: Detail the TIBA procedures with respect to VHF frequencies to be used
concept: TIBA (Traffic Information Broadcast by Aircraft) is a procedure used when ATS facilities lose communication capability. When ATS fails and TIBA is activated, pilots broadcast traffic information to other aircraft on designated VHF frequencies. In New Zealand, the primary TIBA frequency is 121.5 MHz (emergency frequency), with backup frequencies depending on the region and time. Pilots tune to the designated TIBA frequency, maintain listening watch, and transmit position reports and traffic information in a standardised format. The AIPNZ details specific TIBA frequencies for each Flight Information Region (FIR) and operating areas. Pilots must consult the AIPNZ and NOTAM for active TIBA procedures and frequencies during ATS facility outages.
exam_tip: TIBA = ATS facility failure. Use 121.5 MHz (primary) or backup frequencies listed in AIPNZ. Check NOTAM for active TIBA procedures.
key_facts: TIBA activated when ATS facilities lose communication capability
Primary TIBA frequency: 121.5 MHz
Backup frequencies listed in AIPNZ by region
Pilots maintain listening watch and transmit position reports
Position reports include: callsign, position, altitude, heading, flight time
AIPNZ and NOTAM detail active TIBA procedures and frequencies
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25 §2.18.4(a), AIPNZ ENR 1.1 SAR, AC172-1

topic: 2.18 — Loss of Communications — ATS Equipment Failure
syllabus_desc: Detail the TIBA procedures with respect to listening watch
concept: During TIBA operations, maintaining a continuous listening watch on the TIBA frequency is mandatory. All aircraft in the affected Flight Information Region must monitor the designated TIBA frequency and be prepared to receive and relay traffic information. Pilots listen for other aircraft position reports and traffic calls to maintain situational awareness. If you hear a traffic report that applies to your flight (e.g., another aircraft at similar altitude nearby), you must acknowledge receipt and adjust course or altitude if necessary to maintain safe separation. The listening watch must be maintained throughout the flight and cannot be delegated. Electronic systems may have backup frequencies; ensure you're monitoring the correct active TIBA frequency by checking NOTAM.
exam_tip: TIBA listening watch: Monitor assigned frequency continuously. Listen for other aircraft, acknowledge traffic reports, maintain separation.
key_facts: Continuous listening watch on TIBA frequency is mandatory
All aircraft in FIR must monitor and be prepared to receive traffic
Listen for position reports from other aircraft
Acknowledge traffic reports that apply to your flight
Adjust course/altitude if necessary to maintain separation
Listening watch cannot be delegated or interrupted
Check NOTAM for correct active TIBA frequency
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25 §2.18.4(b), AIPNZ ENR 1.1 SAR, AC172-1

topic: 2.18 — Loss of Communications — ATS Equipment Failure
syllabus_desc: Detail the TIBA procedures with respect to times of broadcasts
concept: During TIBA operations, pilots transmit position reports and traffic information on a fixed schedule to reduce congestion and ensure orderly communication. Standard TIBA procedures typically require position reports every 30 minutes or at specified waypoints, depending on the route and airspace. The AIPNZ and active NOTAM specify the broadcast schedule, intervals, and any special reporting requirements. Traffic information (e.g., 'Traffic southbound at 3,000 feet') should be transmitted as soon as observed but without disrupting position report timing. Pilots must time their transmissions to avoid overlapping with other aircraft and ensure the frequency is clear before transmitting. In busy areas, ATC may assign staggered reporting times to prevent frequency congestion.
exam_tip: TIBA broadcasts: Position reports every 30 mins (check NOTAM for exact intervals). Traffic info broadcast immediately when observed. Check frequency is clear before transmitting.
key_facts: Position reports transmitted on fixed schedule (typically every 30 minutes)
AIPNZ and NOTAM specify broadcast times and intervals
Traffic information transmitted immediately upon observation
Listen before transmitting to avoid overlapping with other aircraft
Staggered reporting times may be assigned by ATC to reduce congestion
Standard position report format: callsign, position, altitude, heading, flight time
source: AC 61-3 Rev 25 §2.18.4(c), AIPNZ ENR 1.1 SAR, AC172-1
